surface: Phases 2–5 of 6 (docs/octane-scan-port-plan.md): the programmatic core — `scan`, `setOptions`, `getOptions`, `getReport`, `resetReport`, `onRender(Component, cb)`, the `log`/`onCommitStart`/`onCommitFinish` options — plus the render-outline overlay (labeled per-component flashes fading per `animationSpeed`, re-measured every frame, pointer-transparent, verified in real Chromium). Plus the toolbar (live render counter, pause/resume, animation-speed cycle) as plain shadow-DOM the profiler cannot see — upstream renders its UI in Preact rather than React for the same self-instrumentation reason. Plus `useScan` (slot-forwarding hook variant) and the click-to-inspect inspector v1 (identity, report counts, and the profiler’s actual schedule causes; Escape exits). Live props/state viewing and the script-tag auto entry are not yet ported; lite-scope DOM over-approximation can blur inspector attribution (see the plan’s follow-ups).
divergences: No fibers: callbacks receive an `OctaneRenderInfo` (component metadata, phase, schedule causes, timings, and a pull-based `domNodes()` resolver) instead of a React `Fiber`.

Enablement is a build property, not a runtime flag: the app must compile with `octane({ profile: true })` — octane's production compile strips the inspection channel entirely, so react-scan's `dangerouslyForceRunInProduction` has no equivalent.
`getReport()` returns per-component aggregates keyed by compile-time component identity rather than fiber-keyed maps.
ssr: Client-only by nature; every export is a safe no-op under SSR because the server produces no profiler events.
